---
title: "Geomatics and Earth Surface Modelling part 1"
author: "Francesco Pirotti"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
classoption: a4paper
papersize: a4
output: 
  slidy_presentation: 
    fig_width: 6
    fig_height: 4
    footer:    "2024, Prof. Francesco Pirotti"
    css: style.css
  pdf_document:
    toc: true 
    number_sections: true  
    fig_width: 6
    fig_height: 4
  html_document:
    toc: true 
    toc_depth: 2
    toc_float:
      collapsed: true
      smooth_scroll: false 
  beamer_presentation: default
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Introduzione ad R {.Section1 .titlepage}
## What we will learn

- structure of R (base and packages), capabilities
- RStudio: how it works (overview)
- documentation, examples, snippets for learning
- main categories of data: objects, variables and functions in R
- variable assignment and use
- reading, processing and writing structured data (tables) and unstructured data
- reading and interpreting *geospatial* data
- representing data with charts and maps

## R: overview

- Is R only for statistical analysis? No!
  - E.g. this material is created with R (see *rmarkdown*)
  - Analyses can become interactive with *Shiny* (e.g. InforSAT, VARCITIES projects...)
  - ...

## R: overview

- Installation of R, versions
- R structure – installation path
![](images/clipboard-431216307.png)


## RStudio

- desktop/server version
- interface advantages:
    - console/terminal/background
    - projects/packages/help
    - Environment/History/Connection/Tutorial
- Saving a project, project folder, GIT and GitHub
- Default Working Directory, use of the tab key to recall paths

![](images/clipboard-3935879104.png){width="6in"}

## Code lines – commands

R is a program based on lines of code with commands that perform
operations on data.

The user enters commands at the prompt ( \> ) and each command is
executed one at a time by pressing return.

Command lines are usually saved in a "script" file with
extension "R" (.R) and are executed one at a time using "enter" or
with multiple selection and "enter".

With RStudio it is possible to run the entire file, optionally
stopping at specific points called "breakpoints" (we will see this during the course).

> Exercise: run the command shown in the figure in the [previous slide](#).
```{r echo=TRUE}
saluto <- "ciao"
print(saluto)
```

## Comments and sections

If you do not want to execute some lines, simply place a hash
character (`#`) at the beginning of the text that you do NOT want to execute.

NB: if inserted at the beginning of the line, followed by text and by 4 or more
characters `-` or `#`, the text becomes a section.

![](images/clipboard-2771987315.png){width="5in"}

### 

## Documentation/help {#sec-documentazione-esempi-snippets-per-imparare}

Each function has extensive documentation with many examples.
Calling a function with one or two question marks opens the
documentation. In most cases the examples can be executed by
copying and pasting them.

> Exercise: run the first example from the documentation of the function
> *print*

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE  }

?print
??print

```
## Packages/Libraries

Many additional features are available through add-on components
that must be installed with the command
`install.packages(<library name>)` and then loaded with the command
`library(<library name>)`. In RStudio they can also be loaded through
the graphical interface.

![](images/clipboard-348530046.png)

## Packages/Libraries

Not all packages are in the CRAN repository; some are on GitHub or
can be downloaded from other sources – see some examples below

-   <https://github.com/fpirotti/rPET>

-   <https://github.com/fpirotti/CloudGeometry>

To install libraries available only outside CRAN you can use a library
called "devtools"

```{r eval=FALSE}
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("fpirotti/CloudGeometry")
```
## Variables and functions

Here we see a variable and a function.

**NB1** – the variable "saluto" is in the *global* Environment
("scope/environment/context"). The function "print" is in the scope
of the "base" package – hold down the CTRL key and click the function
name to see what happens.

**NB2** – assignment operator `<-` (or `<<-` if you want
to force assignment to a *global* variable)

![](images/clipboard-3935879104.png)

## Variables and functions: Scope

\*Variables created outside functions are known as
***global variables***; they can be used both inside
functions and outside them.

Below we create our own function "salutami" that performs the
greeting. Try modifying the assignment operator from `<-` to
`<<-` and run it again!!

```{r eval=FALSE, include=TRUE}

greet <- function(){
  greeting <- "hello!!!"
  print(greeting)
}

print(greeting)
greet()
print(greeting)


```
## Data structures in R

NB: every element in R is considered (and actually is) a VECTOR. R
functions treat every variable as a vector. What does this mean? It
means that functions process all elements of a vector "by default" and
that each element can be indexed with a number starting from 1 (not
from 0 as is usually the case in many other programming languages).

-   vector
-   character
-   integer
-   numeric

```{r   echo=T,  eval=FALSE, comment=F }

miaVar <- FALSE
class(miaVar)
miaVar[[1]]
miaVar[[2]]

```

## Data type: vectors

Why does what you see below happen (*numeric* data become
*character*)?

```{r  }

miaVar <- c(1,4,6,8)
class(miaVar)
miaVar[[1]]
miaVar[[2]]

miaVar[[2]] <- "evviva"
class(miaVar)
print(miaVar)

```

## Data Type: matrix

A **matrix** is an object with a matrix structure, meaning it is two-dimensional (**rows × columns**); think of it as a group of vectors stacked on top of or alongside each other.



Values are accessed and assigned using `[r, c]`, where `r` and `c` are the **row and column indices**. You can leave an index blank to access the entire row or column.

```{r}
mat <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2, ncol = 2)
mat[[2]]
mat[1,2]
mat[1,]
mat[1,2] <- 100


```
## Data Type: array

An **array** is a multidimensional matrix.

r=rows, c=columns, m=matrix.... etc...

> Exercise: see below how to create a 3-dimensional array. Note that
> it duplicates 9 values 2 times. Try providing 8 values instead of nine. Try
> providing 3 values. What happens?

```{r}
 
# 2 vectors 
values1 <- c(5, 9, 3) 
values2 <- c(10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15) 
column.names <- c("C1", "C2", "C3") 
row.names <- c("R1", "R2", "R3") 
matrix.names <- c("Matrix1", "Matrix2") 
  
# 3d array  
arr <- array(c(values1, values2), dim = c(3, 3, 2), 
                  dimnames = list(row.names, 
                                  column.names, 
                                  matrix.names)) 
print(arr) 

```

## Tipo dati: List

Le strutture vector/matrix/array, possono contenere solo una tipologia
base (numeric, integer, character, logical...). Ma la struttura LIST no!

La struttura *list* è un set di dati eterogenei; opzionalmente, è
possibile assegnare dei nomi a ciascun elemento nel set.


---
title: "Data Types in R: Lists and Data Frames"
output: html_document
---

```{r}
# Example of list creation and manipulation
my_list <- list(1, 4, 6, 8)
class(my_list)
my_list[[1]]
my_list[[2]]

# Lists can hold heterogeneous data types
my_list[[2]] <- "hurray"
class(my_list)
my_list[[1]]
my_list[[2]]
print(my_list)
```

## Data Type: Named Lists

Note: A **LIST** structure is essentially a set or "list" of objects associated with an index. While the index is typically an integer, it can also be a text string (similar to the concept of "key-value" pairs).

A key/index can be assigned to reference elements within the list:

```{r}
# Assigning names to list elements
names(my_list)
names(my_list) <- c("first", "second")

# Accessing by index vs accessing by name
my_list[[1]]
my_list[["first"]]
```

**Disadvantages of list structures:** They use more memory!

> **Note on memory allocation:** If you need to handle large volumes of data, consider **pre-allocating** memory IF you know the final size. See the blog post [here](https://blog.sellorm.com/2019/12/16/vector-pre-allocation-in-r-a-simple-example/) for more details.

```{r}
# Pre-allocating a large numeric vector
veryLargeVector = numeric(1000)
veryLargeVector[[100]]
```

## Data Type: Data Frame

A **data frame** is a list-type structure where every "column" (attribute) must have an equal number of "rows." Data frames allow for manipulation by filtering rows and operating on columns.

Both rows and columns can have unique identifiers.

```{r}
# Creating a data frame from vectors
c.lat <- c(45.1, 45.2, 45.3)
c.lon <- c(11.1, 11.2, 11.3)

df.geo.stz <- data.frame(stz = c("A", "B", "C"),
                         longitude = c.lon,
                         latitude = c.lat)

# Checking and modifying names
colnames(df.geo.stz)
rownames(df.geo.stz)

# Assigning station names as row names
rownames(df.geo.stz) <- df.geo.stz$stz
rownames(df.geo.stz)
```  

## Data Type: Data frame

Columns are **vectors** — you can call and assign values to a column using `$`, `[[]]`, `[,"<columnname>"]`, or `[row, "<columnname>"]`.

As with [Data Type: matrix], you can use **row/column indices**.

```{r eval= FALSE }
df.geo.stz$stz
df.geo.stz[,"stz"]
df.geo.stz[["stz"]]
df.geo.stz$stz <- c("A1","B1", "C1")

```


## Data Type: Tibble

A specific type of data frame, which we will encounter when using the "tidyverse" library infrastructure. Advantages:

- It does not modify names by replacing spaces with dots (See example).

- It never uses `row.names()`. The goal of the *tidyverse* is to store variables consistently. Therefore, it never stores a variable as a special attribute (i.e., row names).

```{r message="FALSE", warning="FALSE"}
library(tidyverse)
geo.stz.tb <- tibble(df.geo.stz)
class(geo.stz.tb)
#geo.stz.tb
names(data.frame(`my column name with space` = 1)) 
names(tibble(`my column name with space` = 1))
```

-   Only recycles vectors of length 1. This is because recycling vectors of greater length is a frequent source of hidden problems. Recycling: try the following commands:

```{r}
v <- 1:10
v[]
v[] <- 1
v[]
v[] <- 1:5
v[]
# not working below!
#v[] <- 1:3

```

## Converting Objects

Compatible objects can be converted; for example, from matrix => data.frame using the command
`as.data.frame(<matrix object>)` conversely, be careful that all vector
types are made homogeneous.

Try the following exercise below.

```{r message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
mat
df.mat <- as.data.frame(mat)
as.matrix(df.mat)
df.mat$nuovaColonna <- "testo"
as.matrix(df.mat)
df.mat
```

## Saving R Objects

RStudio can save the entire project, including the variables and functions you see in the top right.

The `save` command saves to a file with the "rda" extension, which is also directly recognized by RStudio. Note: it is possible to save any object, even functions!

Try clicking on the file after running the first line of the command below!

```{r} 
newobj <- "una stringa"
save(df.geo.stz, newobj, file="oggetti.rda")
load("oggetti.rda")
saveRDS(df.geo.stz, file = "geo.stz.RDS") 
df.geo.stz <- readRDS("geo.stz.RDS") 
```
 ## R Operators {#sec-gli-operatori-di-r}

R's arithmetic and logical operators work on both individual scalars
and on vectors and structures like *array* and *matrix*.

Note: this means that operations can be performed on all the
individual values within the structure internally (*vectorization*)!

```{r results='hide'}
c.lat + 10
c.lat == c.lon

c.lat[[1]] <- 44
c.lat <- c.lat - 1
## AND / OR
c.lat == c.lon & c.lat==0
c.lat == c.lon | c.lat==45.1
df.mat$V3 <- df.mat$V2**2
#c.lat == c.lon && c.lat==c.lat
#c.lat == c.lon || c.lat==c.lat 
```
 # Data in/process/out {.Section1 .titlepage}

## What we will do

We will learn to read, transform, and write data from different sources:

* Structured text files (**CSV**)
* Spreadsheets (**XLSX**)
* Unstructured online data such as **JSON**

## Data Input: CSV

CSV files are structured text files formatted as tables and are widely used for data sharing. The standard command is `read.csv`. 

**Note:** it is not optimized for efficiency. For massive datasets, use the `fread` command from the `data.table` library.

```{r csv_input,  echo=TRUE}
# Load data using standard and data.table methods
# Note: This assumes you have a 'data' folder
prec <- read.csv("data/Prec_mensili_2020.csv")
prec2 <- data.table::fread("data/Prec_mensili_2020.csv")
summary(prec2)
```

## Data Input: Excel Sheets

Spreadsheets in MS Excel format are more structured than CSV files. To read/write this data, additional libraries are required.

```{r excel_input, eval=FALSE}
prec3 <- prec2
## Create a new calculated column 
prec3$norm2020 <- prec3$`2020 (mm)` / prec3$`media 1993-2019 (mm)`

# Write to Excel with multiple sheets
writexl::write_xlsx(list("original" = prec2, "processed" = prec3), path = "data/output.xlsx")

# Read back from Excel
prec4 <- readxl::read_xlsx("data/output.xlsx")
```



![](images/clipboard-2827995654.png)
## Data Input: JSON

JSON (**J**ava**S**cript **O**bject **N**otation) is used for transferring unstructured data. We will use data directly from the ARPAV API.

```{r json_input, eval=FALSE}
## Download directly from ARPAV API
api.url <- "[https://api.arpa.veneto.it/REST/v1/meteo_meteogrammi?rete=MGRAMMI&coordcd=18&orario=0&rnd=0.9966091913301682](https://api.arpa.veneto.it/REST/v1/meteo_meteogrammi?rete=MGRAMMI&coordcd=18&orario=0&rnd=0.9966091913301682)"
stazioni.meteo <- jsonlite::read_json(api.url)
```


## Basic plotting

La funzione `plot` riesce a creare grafici di base. Andiamo a
confrontare le precipitazioni del 2020 con quelle medie 1993-2019.

```{r, eval=FALSE, fig.height=6, fig.width=6 }
prec3 <- prec2
plot(prec3$`2020 (mm)`[1:12], prec3$`media 1993-2019 (mm)`[1:12], xlab="Prec. 2020 (mm)")
abline(1,1)
```

## Grafici di base

Miglioriamo un po' il grafico - ma utilizzeremo poi la libreria ggplot2
per ottenere grafici migliori.

```{r, eval=FALSE, fig.height=6, fig.width=6}
plot(prec3$`2020 (mm)`[1:12], prec3$`media 1993-2019 (mm)`[1:12], 
     xlab="Prec. 2020 (mm)", ylab="Prec.media  (mm)",
     xlim=c(0,300), ylim=c(0,300))
abline(1,1)
```
 
# Data Transformation {.Section1 .titlepage}

## Introduction

Modifying data means adding, removing, filtering, and manipulating
colonne, creare sommari e statistiche di interesse aggregando dati.
In short, the data is being "manipulated." In English, the term
"data wrangling" is also used.

We will "train" using the ARPAV weather station JSON data downloaded 
from the JSON.

::: Objective
**Objective**: create a graph with the position of the stations and
add values by joining with other data.
:::
## Iterators

It is fundamental to know how to perform operations on all elements of an object (e.g., all columns of a *data.frame*, all rows of a *data.frame*, all objects in a *list*).

```{r results='hide'}
dati <- stazioni.meteo$data
length(dati)
## LOOP classico: visto male per 
## performance bassa, 
## ma non necessariamente vero
for(i in 1:length(dati)){
  print(dati[[i]])
  break # exits the loop
  next #  go to next cycle of iteration
  print("This line will not be evaluated due to 'next'")
}

funz <- function(x){
  length(x)
}
# lapply/mapply/sapply/apply for rows/columns 
# remove the comment from below to run '''lapply'''
# lapply(dati, funz)  


```
# Tidyverse {.Section1 .titlepage}

## Introduction

From the [Tidyverse website](https://www.tidyverse.org/): "The tidyverse is an opinionated collection of R packages designed for data science. All packages share an underlying design philosophy, grammar, and data structures."

The grammar for data manipulation provides a coherent set of functions (verbs) from the [*dplyr*](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/) library:

-   **`mutate()`** adds new variables that are functions of existing variables.
-   **`select()`** picks variables based on their names.
-   **`filter()`** picks cases based on their values.
-   **`summarise()`** reduces multiple values to a single summary.
-   **`arrange()`** changes the ordering of the rows.

By combining these with **`group_by()`**, operations are stratified "by group" for aggregated analysis. There are many other operators, but these are the core verbs.



## Iterators: map

The "map" operator is similar to the "apply" family but with more functionality. By using **map_df**, the results are automatically collected into a table (tibble)!

```{r message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)

# Let's assume weather_stations$data is a list of lists from a JSON source
# map_df creates a data frame by row-binding the elements of the list

# Example of usage (conceptual based on previous context):
# dtf <- map_df(.x = weather_stations$data, .f = function(x){ 
#   # Transformation logic here 
#   return(as.data.frame(x))
# })
```
    
    ## Iterators: map

NOTE that `map` collects each element of the list into a vector, and a vector can only contain values of a single base type (*character, numeric, integer, logical...*). It will always choose *character* if it encounters mixed types.

**Note:** with `map_df` this does not happen because the `map_df` function is "smart" and understands which type to use for each column. EXCEPT for the "value" column. Why?**

** Note that in the JSON, the number in "value" is inside quotes, therefore it is treated as text (character) and not as numeric. Compare this with latitude and longitude!

```{r}
# Example of the difference in data typing
# Latitude/Longitude in JSON: 11.12 (numeric)
# Value in JSON: "15.5" (character)

# To fix this after map_df:
# dtf$valore <- as.numeric(dtf$valore)
```

 ## Filter and Select

Operations using **tidyverse** (and other systems) use process concatenation operators. In this case, the pipe operator `%>%` is used, which indicates that the output of one process should be used as the input for the next process.

Here is an example of a filter operation followed by column selection:



```{r eval=FALSE}
## Select only the stations in the province of Belluno
dtf.belluno <- dtf %>% filter(provincia == "BELLUNO")

## Adding filter criteria separated by a comma is equivalent to using AND 
dtf %>% filter(provincia != "BELLUNO", valore < 10)
dtf %>% filter(provincia != "BELLUNO" & valore < 10)

## Using the OR operator (|) and then selecting specific columns
dtf %>% filter(provincia == "BELLUNO" | valore < 12) %>% 
  select(nome_stazione, valore)
```

## Mutate

The `mutate` command allows you to calculate new columns based on existing ones.



```{r eval=FALSE}
## Create a new column (or overwrite) with the square of the value
dtf %>% 
  mutate(valore = valore^2) %>% 
  select(valore)
```

## Summary Table of Main Verbs

| Function | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `filter()` | Selects rows based on their values |
| `select()` | Selects columns based on their names |
| `mutate()` | Creates or transforms columns |
| `%>%` | Pipes (passes) the result to the next function |
| `%|%` | Pipes (passes) the result to the next function |

## Aggregations

Aggregation operations using `group_by` and `summarise`.



**Warning:** the `mean` function returns `NA` if it finds any `NA` values among the data. To prevent this behavior, use the argument `na.rm = TRUE`.

```{r}
# This will return NA if any missing values are present
dtf %>% 
  group_by(tipo) %>% 
  summarise(valore = mean(valore))

# This correctly calculates the mean excluding NAs
dtf %>% 
  group_by(tipo) %>% 
  summarise(valore = mean(valore, na.rm = TRUE))
```

## Joining tables (join)

Joins are well-known operations in data management; they are used to horizontally combine values from different tables using one or more columns with matching values.



```{r eval=FALSE}
# Example of a Left Join
# We keep all records from the first table and add columns from the second 
# where there is a match in 'codice_stazione'
dt_combined <- dtf %>% 
  left_join(other_data, by = c("codice_stazione" = "station_id"))
```

### Main Join Types

- **`left_join()`**: Keeps all observations in the left table.
- **`right_join()`**: Keeps all observations in the right table.
- **`inner_join()`**: Keeps only observations present in both tables.
- **`full_join()`**: Keeps all observations present in either table.


![](images/clipboard-420454527.png)

![](images/clipboard-2456461929.png)

## Unione tabelle (join)

Vediamo un esempio pratico: andiamo ad unire i valori di oggi a quelli
di alcuni giorni fa alle nostre stazioni. [Scarica qui il dato
CSV](https://www.cirgeo.unipd.it/archivio/shared/R/ARPA2024_corsoBase/dati/ARPA_temperatura_20240528.csv).

```{r}
dt.past <- read.csv("dati/ARPA_temperatura_20240528.csv")
dt.past <- dt.past %>% select(statnm, statcd, dataora, media)
dt.joined <- dt.past %>%  left_join(dtf, by=c("statcd"="codice_stazione") ) 
```

## Esercizio 2

-   Esportare un foglio excel ed un file CSV separato da TAB con i dati
    di precipitazione in Veneto fatti fino a questo punto (df.joined) ed
    un file di R che li legge in 2 oggetti tibble.

-   consegnate: i seguenti file:

    -   *es2_cognome_nome.xlsx*

    -   *es2_cognome_nome.csv*

    -   *es2_cognome_nome.R*

## Ristrutturazione: wide=\>long

Questo tipo di modifica è molto comune per rappresentare i dati in modo
differente. Vediamo un esempio pratico:

```{r}
## andiamo a creare classi di temperatura
classi <- cut(dtf$valore, breaks = c(-10,0,10,20,30))
## mappatura con tabella a doppia entrata
tb.wide <- table(dtf$provincia, classi)
## nota che è un oggetto "table", trasformiomo in data.frame
tb.wide <- as.data.frame.matrix(tb.wide)
rownames(tb.wide)
## nota ancora che le provincie sono rownames - se converto 
## a tibble non ci sono pià 
rownames(as_tibble(tb.wide) )
## creo colonna e relacate la mette all'inizio (provate senza "relocate")
tb.wide <- tb.wide %>% mutate(Provincie = rownames(tb.wide))%>%  relocate(Provincie)
## tabella con numero di stazioni per classe espressa "wide" 
tb.wide

## trasformo 
tb.long <- tb.wide  %>% pivot_longer(!Provincie, 
    names_to = "Classe Temp. °C", values_to = "Numero"
    )
tb.long

```

## Ristrutturazione: long=\>wide

Operazione inversa per tornare alla tabella di origine

```{r}

tb.long2wide <- tb.long  %>% pivot_wider( 
  names_from = "Classe Temp. °C", values_from = "Numero"
  )
tb.long2wide
```

# Grafici {.Section1 .titlepage}

## Plots with ggplot2 - Scatter Plot

The basic "`plot`" functions are useful, but there are libraries that
provide more functionality. Let's introduce "ggplot2". Note that a
graph is created by concatenating plot objects using the "+"
operator. "`aes`" stands for *aesthetics* and indicates colors and
axis values, aggregations, etc.


```{r fig.height=4, fig.width=8, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(dtf, aes(x=quota, y=as.numeric(valore))) + geom_point( ) + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")+ theme_bw()
```

## Plots with GGplot - Box and Whisker

Note: the "as.numeric" part is necessary because the column, even
though it contains numbers, is of the "character" type. Let's modify
it with "as.numeric", but you will see two missing values
(NA=not available).

```{r warning=FALSE}
dtf$valore <- as.numeric(dtf$valore)
ggplot(dtf) + geom_boxplot(aes(x=tipo, y=valore )) + theme_bw()
```

## Multiple Plots (facets)

`facet_wrap` and `facet_grid` allow you to "explode" a plot based on one
or more grouping variables. Here is an example using an estimate of the
frequency distribution function.

```{r   warning=FALSE}
ggplot(dtf) + geom_density(aes(x=valore )) + theme_bw()
ggplot(dtf) + geom_density(aes(x=valore)) + facet_wrap(vars(tipo)) + theme_bw() 
```

## Plats with facets

`facet_wrap` e `facet_grid` consentono di "esplodere" per una o più
variabili di raggruppamento.

```{r fig.height=16, fig.width=6, warning=FALSE}
 ggplot(dtf) + geom_density(aes(x=valore)) + facet_grid(provincia~tipo ) + theme_bw()
```

## Geo Data and Graphics

Do we have columns with coordinates? Can we create a graph with a
territorial background and treat certain columns as coordinates? For
geo data, we use the *sf* and *terra* libraries. They are not
indispensable for geostatistics, but they are highly recommended.

```{r fig.height=5, fig.width=6, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(ggspatial, quietly = T)
library(sf)  ## se non l'avete installata non vi preoccupate 
theme_set(theme_bw())
ggplot(dtf) + geom_point(aes(x=longitudine, y=latitudine, color=valore, size=valore )) 
dtf.geo <- sf::st_as_sf(dtf,coords = c("longitudine","latitudine"), crs=4326 )
ggplot(dtf.geo) + geom_sf(aes(color=valore, size=valore )) + coord_sf()
```

## Graphs and PNG Exports

The method for exporting graphs as images or PDFs:

```{r eval=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
png("graphicsFile.png")

 p <- ggplot(dtf.geo) + geom_sf(aes(color=valore, size=valore )) + coord_sf()
 print(p)
dev.off()
```

We can set dimensions in pixels (width and height) and also the
resolution (res)... try it out and see.

```{r  eval=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
png("file300dpi.png", width=2000, height=2000, res=300)   
  p <- ggplot(dtf.geo) + geom_sf(aes(color=valore, size=valore )) + coord_sf()  
  print(p) 
dev.off()
```

## Graphs and PDF Exports

The PDF format has the advantage/disadvantage of being vector-based (it
does not lose definition) — try checking the resolution yourself. Why
don't we use PDF ALL the time? Imagine inserting millions of points... it
loses efficiency.


```{r eval=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
pdf("fileGrafico.pdf")  
  p <- ggplot(dtf.geo) + geom_sf(aes(color=valore, size=valore )) + coord_sf()  
  print(p) 
dev.off()
```

NB possiamo creare un iterazione per fare un grafico per foglio in un
singolo file PDF!

```{r eval=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

plotMe <- function(tipoStazione){
  dati <- dtf.geo %>% filter(tipo==tipoStazione) 
  p <- ggplot(dati) + geom_sf(aes(color=valore)) + ggtitle(paste("Tipo: ", tipoStazione)) + coord_sf() 
  p
}
## NB la colonna "tipo" è di tipo FACTOR
pdf("fileGraficoMulti.pdf")  
    for(tipoStz in levels(dtf.geo$tipo) ){
      p <- plotMe(tipoStz)
      print(p)
    } 
dev.off()
```

## Cheatsheets

[Sono PDF formato poster con indicazioni di base sull'utilizzo di
strumenti R](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/)

![](images/clipboard-1740990708.png)
